title: Stream Processing Theory — Watermarks, Event-Time, Windows & Exactly-Once Semantics
subtitle: The theoretical foundations behind Apache Flink, Kafka Streams, and Google Dataflow — and how SAP can evolve from batch ETL to real-time streaming

sections.content:
## The Stream Processing Revolution

Traditional data processing is **batch**: collect data for an hour, run a job, store results. This works for overnight reporting but fails for real-time fraud detection, live dashboards, and event-driven architectures.

**Stream processing** flips the model: process each event as it arrives, maintain continuous state, and emit results with sub-second latency. But this introduces fundamental challenges:

- **Ordering** — Events may arrive out of order (network delays, retries)
- **Completeness** — When is a window "done"? What if a late event arrives?
- **Consistency** — How do you ensure every event is processed exactly once, even during failures?
- **State** — How do you maintain gigabytes of state across 1000 parallel operators?

This article covers the theoretical foundations that every Staff+ engineer needs for streaming systems. For SAP architects: these concepts map directly to SAP Data Intelligence, SAP Event Mesh, and the future of real-time SAP analytics.

cards.title: Event Time vs Processing Time
cards.body:
**Event time** is when the event *actually happened* (timestamp embedded in the event).

**Processing time** is when the event is *processed* by the streaming system.

**The gap between them is unbounded.** An event may be delayed by network issues, retries, or upstream batch processing. Processing-time-based windows give wrong results during delays.

**Google Dataflow / Apache Beam** introduced event-time processing as a first-class concept. Apache Flink and Kafka Streams now support it natively. **Always use event-time for correctness-sensitive operations.**

cards.title: Watermarks — The Heart of Event-Time Processing
cards.body:
A **watermark** is a timestamp assertion: "I am now confident that no more events with timestamp <= W will arrive." Watermarks tell the streaming system when it's safe to close a window and emit results.

**Perfect watermark:** Impossible in practice (you'd need to know the future).

**Heuristic watermark:** Based on observed lateness patterns. E.g., "95th percentile of observed delay is 30s, so watermark = min(observed timestamps) - 30s."

**Idle watermark:** If a partition has no data, the watermark stalls. Solution: advance watermarks on idle partitions (Punctuated / Periodic watermarks in Flink).

cards.title: Watermark Propagation Across Operators
cards.body:
In a DAG of streaming operators, watermarks flow from sources to sinks. Each operator:

1. Receives watermarks from all its input partitions
2. Advances its own watermark to `min(watermarks_from_all_inputs)`
3. Emits the updated watermark to downstream operators

**Key implication:** A slow partition blocks the entire pipeline's watermark. If one Kafka partition has no data, its watermark never advances, preventing window emission everywhere.

**Mitigation:** Idle-source detection — advance watermark for partitions that haven't produced events for N seconds.

cards.title: Allowed Lateness & Side Outputs
cards.body:
Even with good watermarks, some events arrive late. **Two strategies:**

1. **Allowed Lateness** — Keep the window open for an additional N seconds after the watermark passes. When a late event arrives, re-emit the window with updated results. Flink's `allowedLateness()`.

2. **Side Outputs** — Route late events to a separate stream for offline correction. The main pipeline remains clean; late events are processed in batch.

**Tradeoff:** Allowed lateness increases memory usage (windows stay open longer). Side outputs increase operational complexity (need a secondary batch pipeline).

sections.title: Windowing Strategies
rows: Overlapping windows of fixed size, sliding by a fixed interval
Rolling averages, moving counts (e.g., 5-min avg, updated every min)
Windows defined by gaps of inactivity (no events for N minutes)
User sessions, clickstreams, IoT device telemetry
Single window per key, must be triggered manually
Accumulating window that grows indefinitely, periodically emitting partial results

sections.content:
## Exactly-Once Semantics — The Three Layers

"Exactly-once processing" is one of the most misunderstood concepts in stream processing. It has three distinct layers:

### Layer 1: Source Exactly-Once
Can the source replay data without duplicates? Kafka does this via consumer offsets. If a Flink job crashes and restarts, it resumes from the last committed offset. **Required condition:** The source must support offset-based replay (Kafka, Kinesis, Pulsar do; plain TCP/UDP sockets can't guarantee this).

### Layer 2: State Exactly-Once
Can the streaming engine update its internal state without duplicates? Flink uses **distributed snapshots (checkpoints)** based on the Chandy-Lamport algorithm. A checkpoint captures:
- Current operator state (key-value maps, lists, aggregates)
- Current source offsets
- Current watermark

If a failure occurs, the entire pipeline rolls back to the last successful checkpoint and replays from that point. **This is why Flink's checkpointing is the foundation of its exactly-once guarantee.**

### Layer 3: Sink Exactly-Once
Can the output sink (database, Kafka topic) accept the same result multiple times without causing duplicates? This requires **idempotent writes** (same input always produces same output) or **transactional sinks** (writes are staged until checkpoint completes, then committed atomically).

**Real-world reality:** Most streaming systems provide Layer 1 + 2 guarantees. Layer 3 requires sink cooperation. Flink's `TwoPhaseCommitSinkFunction` implements a transactional sink using a pre-commit / commit protocol.

sections.title: Flink Checkpointing — Chandy-Lamport Distributed Snapshot
sections.code:
-- Flink checkpointing timeline --

Barrier alignment (exactly-once):

Source[0]  --[1][2][3][B]---> Map[0] --[1][2][B]---> Sink
Source[1]  --[4][5][B]------>         
            ^ barrier injected at source

1. Barrier N is injected into each source partition
2. Each operator, upon receiving barrier N from ALL inputs:
   - Snapshot its current state
   - Forward barrier N to all outputs
3. All operators complete snapshot -> checkpoint N is finalized
4. If failure occurs before checkpoint N+1, roll back to N

At-least-once (vs exactly-once):
- Barriers don't align (no waiting for all inputs)
- Lower latency, potential duplicates
- Use for pipelines where occasional duplicates are acceptable

sections.content:
## SAP Bridge — Stream Processing for the Enterprise

### Current SAP Streaming Landscape

SAP has multiple streaming-related products:

- **SAP Data Intelligence** — Pipeline orchestration with some streaming support (based on Apache Spark streaming and SAP HANA streaming analytics)
- **SAP Event Mesh** — Managed event broker (RabbitMQ/Kafka-compatible) for SAP integration
- **SAP HANA Streaming Analytics** (legacy) — CCL-based continuous computation, being phased out
- **ABAP Push Channels (APC)** — WebSocket-based real-time push for Fiori UIs

### Where Stream Processing Fits in SAP

| Use Case | Today's Approach | Stream Processing Alternative |
|---|---|---|
| Real-time inventory | Periodic batch (every 15 min) | Streaming aggregation per SKU/location |
| Fraud detection in SAP S/4HANA | Post-processing rules | Event-time windowing with ML scoring |
| SAP Convergent Charging | Batch rating jobs | Streaming per-event rating |
| SAP Customer Activity Repository | Daily batch ETL | Real-time event enrichment |
| SAP Cloud ALM monitoring | Polling-based | Streaming metrics pipeline |

### The Skill Translation

SAP architects who understand event-time processing, watermarks, and exactly-once semantics can design streaming pipelines that FAANG companies pay premiums for. The concepts are directly portable:

- **SAP Event Mesh partitioning** → Kafka partition parallelism
- **SAP Data Intelligence operators** → Flink operator DAGs
- **SAP HANA stream tables** → Flink dynamic tables
- **ABAP event-driven architecture via RFC/BAPI** → event-driven microservices with Kafka Streams

sections.title: Stream Processing FAQ for Staff+ Interviews

tabs.label: Watermark Design
tabs.content:
**Interviewer:** "Design a stream processing system for real-time fraud detection. How do you handle late-arriving transactions?"

**Staff+ answer:** "I'd use event-time processing with a **BoundedOutOfOrderness watermark** set to 30 seconds (covering 99th percentile network delay). Windows are 1-minute tumbling with **allowed lateness** of 1 minute — if a transaction arrives up to 90 seconds late, it's still included in the correct window. Transactions arriving after 90 seconds go to a **side output** for batch re-evaluation.

The key design decision is the watermark bound. Too tight (5s): many false positives for late events. Too loose (5min): delayed fraud detection and more state to keep. The bound should come from historical data analysis — measure actual event-time vs processing-time delta for 30 days."

**Why this impresses:** You gave a concrete design with specific numbers (30s bound, 1min allowed lateness, 90s side output cutoff), justification for each number, and a data-driven approach to setting the bound.

tabs.label: Exactly-Once
tabs.content:
**Interviewer:** "How do you guarantee exactly-once processing in a streaming pipeline?"

**Staff+ answer:** "Exactly-once requires three conditions:

1. **Replayable source** — Kafka with committed offsets, so the source can re-read from any point.
2. **Idempotent state updates** — Flink's checkpointing with Chandy-Lamport barriers ensures state is snapshotted exactly once per epoch.
3. **Transactional sink** — A TwoPhaseCommitSinkFunction that buffers writes, commits on checkpoint completion, and aborts on failure.

**The practical challenge** is Layer 3. Many sinks (file systems, most databases) don't support 2PC. Workarounds include:
- **Idempotent writes** with dedup IDs (PostgreSQL INSERT ... ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING)
- **Kafka sink** (Flink writes to Kafka, which *is* replayable, then a secondary consumer batch-loads to the real sink)
- **Upsert semantics** (same data overwrites, no duplicates)"

**Why this impresses:** You enumerated the three conditions precisely, gave the Flink mechanism (Chandy-Lamport, 2PC sink), and provided practical workarounds for when perfect exactly-once is impossible.

tabs.label: SAP Migration
tabs.content:
**Interviewer:** "Migrate SAP's batch ETL pipeline to streaming. What are the challenges?"

**Staff+ answer:** "Three primary challenges:

**1. Data completeness mindset.** SAP accountants expect a closed book — 'all November transactions are final.' Streaming gives approximate results with late data corrections. The solution: hybrid model — streaming for real-time dashboards (preliminary), batch at month-end for final numbers.

**2. State size.** An SAP financial consolidation pipeline processes millions of GL line items. Streaming requires keeping all open-period line items in state. With Flink's RocksDB backend, this is feasible up to TB-scale, but requires careful key design (avoid hot keys per company code).

**3. Schema evolution.** SAP tables evolve with notes and upgrades. Streaming pipelines are sensitive to schema changes — a new field in the source table requires updating the Flink job, reprocessing, or using schema-on-read patterns with Avro/Protobuf schema registries."

**Why this impresses:** You addressed non-technical challenges (SAP accounting's 'closed book' requirement) alongside technical ones. You showed understanding that streaming in an SAP context requires cultural change, not just technology adoption.

tabs.label: Scaling
tabs.content:
**Interviewer:** "Your streaming pipeline has a hot key (e.g., a viral product with 100x normal traffic). How do you handle it?"

**Staff+ answer:** "Hot keys break parallelism because all events for the same key go to the same operator subtask. Solutions:

**1. Key salting** — Add a random suffix to the hot key (e.g., product_123_0, product_123_1, ... product_123_99). Process in parallel, then merge results with an additional operator. Works for simple aggregates (count, sum) but not for stateful operations that depend on key ordering.

**2. Mini-batching within the hot operator** — Accumulate events in a buffer (e.g., 1000 events or 100ms), batch-process them. Reduces per-event overhead for the hot key.

**3. Split the pipeline** — Route hot keys to a dedicated, high-parallelism pipeline. Cold keys go to a normal pipeline. This isolates hotspots from the main pipeline.

**Detection:** Monitor `currentProcessingTime` of each subtask. If one subtask's processing lag grows linearly, it's hitting a hot key. The 99th percentile lag metric is the best indicator."

**Why this impresses:** You gave three distinct strategies with tradeoffs (not just 'salt the key'). You included the detection mechanism (processing lag monitoring) and the operational response. Shows you've dealt with this in production.

events.title: Apache Storm
events.description: Nathan Marz builds Storm at Twitter. First widely-used stream processing system. At-least-once semantics, record-by-record processing. Weak consistency, no event-time support.

events.title: Google Dataflow Paper
events.description: Tyler Akidau et al. publish 'The Dataflow Model.' Formalizes event-time, watermarks, windows, and triggers. The foundational paper for modern stream processing.

events.title: Apache Flink Rising
events.description: Flink gains traction as the first open-source system implementing the Dataflow model natively. Introduces distributed snapshots (Chandy-Lamport) for exactly-once state.

events.title: Kafka Streams
events.description: Confluent launches Kafka Streams — stream processing as a library (no cluster needed). Targets Kafka-native users with simpler deployment than Flink.

events.title: Streaming SQL Standardization
events.description: Flink SQL, ksqlDB, and Beam SQL converge on SQL-based stream processing. Event-time windows expressed via standard SQL TUMBLE/HOP/SESSION functions.

events.title: Unified Batch + Streaming
events.description: Flink 2.0 focuses on making batch a special case of streaming (bounded stream). Spark Structured Streaming closes the gap. The batch/streaming dichotomy disappears.

cards.title: Event-Time Over Processing-Time
cards.body: Always process based on when events happened, not when they arrived. Watermarks bound the uncertainty. Event-time processing is non-negotiable for correctness.

cards.title: Watermarks Drive Correctness
cards.body: Watermarks tell the system when it's safe to emit results. Heuristic watermarks based on observed latency are the production standard. Idle-source detection prevents watermark stalls.

cards.title: Exactly-Once Is Three-Layer
cards.body: Source replayability + state checkpointing + idempotent/transactional sinks = exactly-once. Missing any one layer gives at-least-once or at-most-once. Know which layer each system provides.

cards.title: State Backends Have Tradeoffs
cards.body: Heap state (fast, small) for GB-scale. RocksDB state (slower, TB-scale) for large state. Choose based on state size per operator, not developer convenience.

cards.title: Hot Keys Break Parallelism
cards.body: All events for the same key go to one subtask. Salting, mini-batching, or pipeline splitting mitigate this. Monitoring per-subtask lag detects it.

cards.title: Allowed Lateness: Friend or Foe
cards.body: Keeping windows open longer increases correctness but also memory and latency. Use allowed lateness sparingly (1-2x the watermark bound). Side outputs for extreme late arrivals.
